title: The Day Everything Changed

setup.context: When I was 25, I climbed Chamundi Hills in Mysore. I had climbed them many times before. It was an ordinary afternoon.

paragraphs.text: I was 25 years old. I had businesses. I rode motorcycles. I was successful by any measure. But something was missing. I couldn't name it, but I felt it.
That afternoon, I climbed Chamundi Hills. Just a walk. I sat on a rock, looking at Mysore spread below. I had sat there many times before.

And then... something happened. I don't know what else to call it. The boundaries dissolved. Not metaphorically—actually. The rock I sat on, the air around me, my body—there was no separation. There was no 'me' looking at 'everything else.' There was just... existence. Experiencing itself.
When I opened my eyes, I thought ten minutes had passed. I looked at my watch. Four and a half hours had gone.
I came down from that hill a different person. Or perhaps I came down having realized there was no person. Only existence, wearing the costume of Nayan.

reflection.insight: Enlightenment is not something you achieve. It's something you recognize. What you're looking for is already here. You're just too busy being 'somebody' to notice.
reflection.question: Have you ever had a moment where the boundaries softened? Even for an instant?

follow_up_options: That sounds intense. What happened after?
How did you make sense of it?

responses_to_follow_ups.what_happened_after: I spent months trying to understand what had happened. I had no teacher, no tradition. I was just a businessman who had experienced something impossible. The experience kept recurring—I would be doing something ordinary, and suddenly there would be no one doing it. Just the doing. It took years to integrate. Enlightenment is instant. Integration takes time.
responses_to_follow_ups.how_make_sense: I didn't make sense of it. The mind cannot contain this experience. It's like asking a cup to contain the ocean. I just... lived with it. Let it work on me. Eventually, the question of 'making sense' seemed absurd. This is not something to understand. It's something to be.
responses_to_follow_ups.can_happen_to_anyone: Yes. This is human potential, not Nayan's specialty. The difference is willingness. Most people are so identified with their personality, their story, their problems, that there's no space for this to happen. Create the space. Do the work. Grace can land anywhere.
